data.title: Meta Platforms: Recent Significant Decline And The Longer-Term Story
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Pershing Square Holdings H2 2025 Letter To Shareholders.

At the end of last year, we initiated a significant position in Meta (META), a company we have followed closely for many years. Alongside Alphabet (GOOGL), another one of our core holdings, Meta is the dominant leader in the highly attractive and secularly fast-growing digital advertising space. The company principally owns and operates a “Family of Apps” (Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp) with an entrenched global user base of over 3.5 billion daily active users. This vast, diverse, and highly engaged audience is the source of a powerful network effect: as more users engage more deeply with Meta’s apps, the apps become incrementally more valuable to both users and advertisers, creating an exceptionally durable competitive advantage. The company’s deep insights into consumer behavior and interests allow advertisers to precisely target specific demographics and intentions, making Meta an indispensable and essential marketing platform for businesses seeking highly effective ad spend.

Our opportunity to invest in Meta arose following the company’s Q3 2025 earnings call in October when it announced it would spend significantly more on AI initiatives in 2026 than investors had expected, leading to a 20% share price decline. As a result, we were able to establish our position in Meta at a very attractive valuation of just 20 times earnings per share, earnings which include the significant losses from its Reality Labs segment that amount to nearly 25% of the company’s overall profit. Excluding the Reality Labs losses, the implied market valuation for Meta’s core advertising business was less than 16 times earnings per share. This valuation represented a significant discount to the S&P 500 for one of the world’s great businesses that is currently growing its revenues in excess of 20% per annum.

In our view, concerns surrounding Meta's elevated spending overlook the transformative potential of AI to accelerate near-term revenue growth and reinforce its long-term competitive moats. We believe Meta is one of the clearest beneficiaries of AI and is already seeing material impact across its core business drivers: content recommendation, user engagement, ad monetization and new product innovation.

Improved AI content recommendation systems are driving material growth in user engagement, especially for the video format with time spent watching videos on Instagram up more than 30% year-over-year. Moreover, as content on Meta's platforms has increasingly broadened from the "social graph" (friends/family/followed accounts) to include a wider range of interest-based content from a diverse group of creators, AI recommendation engines enable the company to better understand, surface, and curate content that users desire. Likewise, AI greatly improves ad monetization by leveraging Meta's rich first-party data to deliver more relevant and targeted ads. Automation capabilities – from the company's Advantage+ suite to its generative AI ad creation tools – facilitate an end-to-end self-serve model for advertisers. We are also encouraged by AI's potential to unlock new commercial use cases and engagement modes. For instance, the rollout of Business AI on Meta's messaging platforms could be a game-changer for businesses to pursue scalable, low-cost customer engagement and wearables like Meta Ray-Bans, which present new avenues to leverage AI interactively in the real world.

We believe the substantial upside potential from AI, both within Meta's compute-constrained core business and in its new initiatives, supports front-loading investments in infrastructure and talent. After Meta's next year's planned spending ramp, we expect the company to resume its rapid long-term earnings growth algorithm. Notably, in its year-end earnings call at the end of January, management stated that the near-term acceleration in revenue growth would allow it to continue to grow operating income in 2026 despite significantly elevated levels of investment spending. In addition, the company noted that losses at the Reality Labs division will peak in 2026 and moderate in future years. Should Meta's high levels of revenue growth slow down or the returns on its AI investments be lower than anticipated, we are confident the company will be able to retain the operational levers – reminiscent of the 2023 "Year of Efficiency" – to moderate expenses, maintain profit margins, and deliver continued strong earnings-per-share growth.

Our investment in Meta highlights our approach of closely following a watchlist of high-quality businesses and waiting patiently for an attractive entry price. Having recently trimmed and sold other holdings, we held sufficient cash to move quickly and invest in a business we have long admired that has experienced a significant decline in its share price in recent months due to near-term concerns, which we do not believe will impact the longer-term Meta story.

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. All investments involve risk, including the loss of principal. Please see accompanying endnotes and disclaimers.

DISCLAIMERS

Limitations of Performance Data

Past performance is not necessarily indicative of future results. All investments involve risk including the loss of principal. This report does not constitute a recommendation, an offer to sell or a solicitation of an offer to purchase any security or investment product. This report contains information and analyses relating to all publicly disclosed equity positions above 50 basis points in the Company's portfolio during 2025 and 2026. Pershing Square may currently or in the future buy, sell, cover or otherwise change the form of its investment in the companies discussed in this report for any reason. Pershing Square hereby disclaims any duty to provide any updates or changes to the information contained here including, without limitation, the manner or type of any Pershing Square investment.

data.title: VT: Still The Best Approach To Passive Investing
data.description:
When Neutral Stops Being Neutral

I think that for most long-term investors, keeping a global neutral base still makes a lot of sense.

I still think that a broad, globally diversified, market-cap-weighted allocation is one of the best ways to invest for investors who want to take advantage of market growth without making it their full-time job.

That said, I also think it's a big mistake to ignore the structural changes that passive investing is causing in the market.

The huge growth of ETFs and indexed strategies has made prices lower (which is a good thing), changed how prices are set, changed the relative importance of active analysis, and, in my opinion, started to make market movements bigger in ways that weren't possible a few decades ago.

This doesn't mean that the passive model isn't working anymore. I don't think that at all. What I believe is that pure neutrality may no longer be as neutral as it seems.

When a larger part of the market buys assets automatically, no matter what the fundamentals are at the time, the dynamics change. And I think that change in structure needs to be taken into account when we think about portfolio allocation.

It seems too soon to give up on global neutrality completely, but it also seems silly to think that nothing has changed and that markets work the same way they did in the 1980s or 1990s.

So, for me, this isn't just a matter of "passive vs. active" or "the Vanguard Total World Stock Index ETF (VT) vs. stock picking."

The main idea here is to know when it makes sense to keep a neutral base as the portfolio's structural core and when it might make sense to change that structure (still within a passive framework) to try to take advantage of distortions that are caused by the growth of passive investing itself.

What Happens When Capital Stops Judging

First, it's one thing to say that passive capital has grown. Now it's a whole different thing to know what happens to the market when more and more money just follows rules instead of making decisions. And I think this is the point that people forget most often when they talk about ETFs.

Price discovery is how markets usually work. Investors look at information, disagree with each other, and then buy or sell based on what they think. This disagreement leads to the price. It is never perfect, but it is the result of judgment, whether we like it or not.

Now that more and more capital flows automatically into indexes, the situation changes a little bit. Not because fundamentals stop being important, but because part of the demand isn't reacting directly to them right now that capital is coming in. And, from where I stand, that change in proportion changes how the system works.

Let me explain the image below, with data from Research Affiliates and Morningstar. The numbers below include U.S. open-ended funds and ETFs, even those that are no longer available. The lines on the chart show how much of the total assets under management are in actively managed U.S. equity funds compared to cap-weighted passively managed U.S. equity funds. In February 2024, passive assets under management (the black line) officially overtook active assets (the blue dashed line). This was a big change in how capital is spread out across U.S. equities.

Think about a time when a business gets better at its fundamentals. In a mostly active market, managers look at the change, change their models, increase their position, and, in the end, the price reacts to that analysis.

Now picture a situation where a lot of capital only buys that company if its index weight goes up. The flow starts to depend on a market capitalization that has already been affected by the price. You can start to see a cycle where size affects flow and flow affects size.

I'm not saying that this automatically makes bubbles, because I don't like that kind of simplification. What I'm trying to say is that when flows become more mechanical, markets tend to be less sensitive to small changes in fundamentals and more sensitive to large amounts of capital coming in and going out.

And this leads us to the idea of elasticity. In a market that is very elastic, small changes in how people see things cause prices to change in the same way because capital is always reevaluating its assumptions.

When there are a lot of passive flows, a large part of demand just follows the rules. When money goes into an ETF, it buys everything in the same amount, even if a certain company's stock is a little more or less expensive at that time.

This doesn't seem good or bad to me; it's just a structural feature of the current system. The passive investor is basically saying that the overall price is already good enough and that the low cost makes up for any small flaws.

That assumption has worked well in the past, but the more capital that works under that assumption, the less capital that is set aside to challenge prices.

Concerning Beta, Cycles, And Amplified Movements

If this change in the structure of capital really does change how the market works, the next question is whether the data shows it.

I think it does. When we look at how beta has acted over the years, especially when we compare active and passive strategies, we see an interesting change that, to me, helps tell this story.

In the 1980s and 1990s, active managers often took on more risk than the market did. It was common to see portfolios with betas over 1.0, which means strategies that amplified movements. They went up more when the market went up. They fell even more when the market went down. In that case, active investors were often the ones who made the cycle seem bigger than it was.

The chart below shows the annual value-weighted averages of market betas for portfolios sorted by ownership type. These betas were estimated using the Fama-French and Carhart (1997) four-factor (FF4) model. They fitted trend lines across the entire sample period to show the larger directional patterns. A beta above 1.0 means that the stock is more sensitive to changes in the overall market, while a beta below 1.0 means that the stock is less exposed.

What we see today looks different. Passive strategies, especially those that are well diversified and weighted by market cap, have sometimes shown high beta behavior, especially when there are strong inflows or outflows.

And this makes sense when you think about how the process works. When the market starts to go up and money flows into broad ETFs, it is divided up among the biggest companies, which are already going up by definition. That extra flow makes the move stronger. Gains bring in more money, which makes gains stronger, and the cycle of amplification begins.

The same logic works in the other direction as well. When capital leaves in large amounts, ETFs sell in the same amount. They don't just sell the weakest company; they sell everything. That can speed up declines that might have happened more slowly in a situation where people had more freedom to make choices.

Once more, I'm not saying that ETFs cause cycles. There have always been phases, and markets have always been cyclical. What I mean is that I think the current system can make those cycles worse, both when things are going well and when they are going badly.

You can see this more cyclical pattern in passive vehicles at certain times when you look at rolling three-year periods that compare the overall performance of active and passive strategies.

For instance, the chart below shows how portfolios with different types of ownership have done over the past three years. The dark areas show times when actively held stocks did better than passively owned stocks. These reversals show how flow-driven mispricing can happen in cycles and how disciplined rebalancing can give you an edge in returns. Over time, structural excesses tend to go back to normal, which is good for investors who put their money in the opposite direction instead of just following the most recent winners.

For example, before the dot-com bubble, broader strategies rode the trend hard. They both felt the same pain when the bubble burst. After that, there were long periods when active and passive performance looked very similar. This changed in the most recent cycle, when passive performance stood out again.

We know that long-term results tend to get closer to each other on average, since the market is the market. But active strategies cost more, which lowers net returns over time.

The point here is not to argue about which is better, active or passive. The point for me is to see that the way the market works has changed as the capital structure behind it has changed.

If movements have really gotten stronger (and I think they have), then the investor who thinks he is just buying the average market may actually be dealing with a more concentrated and flow-sensitive dynamic than he thinks.

If the neutral mechanism can make movements stronger, does it make sense to only use it as a strategy?

Passive Does Not Have To Mean Neutral

If everything I've said so far makes sense, the next step is to figure out what to do with that information.

If the market has structurally changed, if passive flows have gained weight, and if this mechanism could make movements stronger, does that mean we should stop using neutral allocation?

No, I don't think so. I still think that keeping a global neutral base is an excellent strategy for an essential part of any given portfolio, especially when it comes to long-term goals, retirement, and building wealth over time. Simplicity, low cost, and a wide range of options are still big advantages.

But I want to add that passive doesn't always mean neutral. A lot of people think those two ideas are the same, but they aren't. You can invest passively, follow simple rules, pay very low fees, and, at the same time, choose not to weight your portfolio strictly by market capitalization.

Equal-weight ETFs are a classic example of this. When you put money into a market-cap-weighted index, the biggest companies get the most weight. When you buy an equal-weight index, you spread the weight out more evenly among the parts.

This means that you are moving some of your money from the biggest companies to the smaller ones in that group. In other words, you're putting more of your money into things that have gotten smaller, things that have been behind, and things that might have more potential but also more risk.

And when we look at historical data from certain sectors, like healthcare or industrials, we can see that equal-weight strategies have made a big difference in returns over long periods of time compared to market-cap-weighted strategies.

You can see a comparison between the healthcare ETF XLV (XLV), which is weighted by market cap, and the equal-weight RSPH (RSPH) below.

And here is the equal-weight RSPN (RSPN) next to the market-cap-weighted XLI (XLI) in the industrial sector.

Don't think this is magic or some secret alpha that no one else has seen. It's just a different kind of risk. You are putting more money into smaller, more unstable companies and less money into the big companies that make up most of the index. In return, you might be able to get premiums that come with that risk profile.

To me, the conversation isn't about giving up global neutrality as the main structure, but whether it makes sense to keep 100% of a portfolio completely neutral, as if the market today were the same as it was forty years ago.

I think it's a good idea to keep a strong neutral base, but I also think it's a good idea to think about conscious tilts, like equal-weight approaches, sector exposure, geographic adjustments, or even stock picking, as long as those choices fit your risk profile and aren't just short-term emotional bets.

Changing a portfolio doesn't mean you have to become a stock picker right away. It just means that being neutral today may not be the same as it was in the past. In the end, I see this as a strategic choice rather than an ideological one.

From Theory To Action And What I Suggest

After hearing all of this about how passive investing has grown, how the market has changed, and how neutrality may not be as neutral as it seems, it only seems fair to share my point of view in a more practical way.

The problem is that my point of view is very clear and almost boring because it is so consistent.

I am always long the Vanguard Total World Stock Index Fund.

I don't wait for the "right moment" or try to time the cycle when it comes to this ETF and the structural base of my portfolio.

And I say that because, in my opinion, VT is a very honest answer to the main problem that most investors face: how to get broad global exposure at a low cost without making the portfolio a second job.

You might also wonder if the iShares MSCI ACWI ETF (ACWI) would be a better choice than VT. Yes, it can, because both invest in a way that is neutral and spreads out around the world, so the main idea behind the strategy stays the same. The fact that their returns have been very similar since they started in 2008 (see chart below) is proof of this.

That being said, we do know that not many passive ETF providers consistently offer expense ratios that are as good as Vanguard's. So, since both offer pretty much the same strategy, I don't see why I should pick the more expensive one. Over the years, even small costs can add up to money you could have made.

Always Buy VT, Even At The Worst Possible Moment

I really think that everyone should have a significant part of their portfolio anchored in a neutral base. To me, VT is the easiest and most effective way to do that.

Not because it is perfect (no ETF is) but because it fixes a lot of problems at once. When you buy one fund, you stop arguing with the world in a way. You've accepted the world as it is now, with the U.S. being the biggest market, emerging markets being smaller, Europe being more mature, and Japan being somewhere in between, and you move on.

And then comes the part that makes people the most uncomfortable. What if I buy when the bubble is at its peak? I really think that question makes sense only when someone is planning on putting all their money into one investment (lump sum), like they're trying to time the market without saying so.

From my point of view, the top only becomes the worst possible time if you buy once, watch it fall, panic, and stop contributing for years, right when the market has become cheaper. If your strategy is structural and you really get that VT is a base that has been around for decades, then the top is just another point on the chart.

For example, the dot-com bubble. Yes, it would have been painful for someone who invested at the peak in 2000 and never put any more money in. But if that same person had kept buying in 2001, 2002, and 2003, when the market was really bad, the average cost would have gone down a lot, and the recovery would have felt a lot less dramatic than it does when you look only at the peak.

And I think this is the main thing that most people neglect when they talk about bubbles. Most stories assume that an investor buys at the top and then goes away, but in reality, a disciplined investor does the opposite: they keep buying, no matter what.

The Math Of Lowering Your Break-Even

Now, let's put this in more objective terms so that it doesn't just seem like a gut feeling. I think it's helpful to go over DCA (dollar-cost averaging) again, even though every investor knows what it is. DCA and passive investing go together like bread and butter, even though it may seem simple and clear.

Picture this: you put $10,000 into the market, and it drops by 50%. Your portfolio drops to $5,000, and it feels like the end of the world. But what you do next is what really matters.

If you stick with the plan and put in another $10,000 at that lower point, everything changes. You have $20,000 in total invested capital, but your portfolio is only worth $15,000 right now because you already had $5,000 and added $10,000 at a much lower price.

For me, this is where average cost really shows how useful it is. You don't have to wait for the market to go back to its old high to break even. You need a much smaller recovery than that because your average cost went down as you kept contributing during the downturn.

When we look back at big problems like 2008 or even 2020, we see that investors who kept putting money in over time had a much shorter path back to break-even than those who just bought once and waited for things to "go back to normal."

I think the biggest mistake is not buying at the top. When the market is cheap, the worst thing you can do is stop your contributions. That's when you're getting a bigger piece of the world for less money, and you're doing it in a neutral, low-cost way, without complicated analysis, just by being disciplined.

Making Neutral Even More Efficient - Here Comes VWO

I also think it's important to be honest about a structural detail of VT. Yes, it is global and passive, but that passiveness has a clear effect: it leads to concentration in the United States.

About 60% of VT is now invested in U.S. stocks. That's not manager bias or anything like that; it's just a sign of how big the U.S. market is compared to the rest of the world. In practice, a 100% VT portfolio is really a 60% U.S. portfolio.

I'm fine with that, and I'm still long VT in any market phase because I'd rather have a wide range of global exposure than give in to the urge to replicate the world myself... manually.

That said, I think it's perfectly fine for an investor to add the Vanguard Emerging Markets Stock Index Fund (VWO) in a meaningful amount, like 30% to 40% of the total portfolio, if they want to make neutrality even more effective. I do this myself, in part because I'm Brazilian and I know how much my market can grow when it goes through a boom.

And the other reason is answered through a very simple math problem, because it helps in seeing the change. If you put 60% of your money in VT and 40% in VWO, you are no longer 60% exposed to the US. It goes down to 36% because 60% of your portfolio is in VT, and about 60% of that is in U.S. stocks.

0.60 x 0.60 = 0.36.

You lower the concentration of the U.S. from 60% to about 36%. From my point of view, that is an interesting structural change because global cycles don't always line up perfectly.

The U.S. market often has weak returns while other parts of the world have stronger ones. Emerging economies, in particular, can go through their own cycles that don't always depend on what's happening on Wall Street.

I don't think this is betting against the US. I see it as more deliberate geographic diversification, still fully within a passive and low-cost framework. To me, that keeps the best part of the strategy while keeping you from being too focused on one country.

Neutral As The Core... And The Rest You Can Tweak

That being said, does it make sense to go against a global neutral allocation and invest only in the region or segment that has fallen behind (like through equal-weight ETFs)?

I don't think the answer is yes or no. I still think that a global neutral base is one of the best ways to get long-term growth with low costs and a wide range of investments. I still think that, for the bulk of a portfolio, a mix of 60% VT and 40% VWO is very hard to beat, especially when it comes to long-term goals like retirement.

I also think that investors need to know what's going on around them. The market today is different from what it was a few decades ago. Passive investing is more important, flows are more mechanical, movements may be bigger, and the logic of market-cap weighting makes concentration in big companies stronger.

If you know the risks and are okay with taking on more volatility as part of your long-term plan, you can tilt the portfolio away from strict neutrality to adjust your exposure while still following a passive strategy.

data.title: LegalZoom: Strong Subscription Growth Defies AI Fears
data.description:
Currently, if there's one emotion that's dominating the stock market, it's fear. Specifically, investors are no longer looking forward to AI as a tailwind in terms of improving business efficiency and eliminating cost but as a threat to existing companies, particularly software companies. The recent release of Claude's Cowork tool that assists in the legal review process sparked a massive selloff in software stocks, and unsurprisingly LegalZoom.com, Inc. (LZ), which is a DIY legal services platform, has been disproportionately hit.

But amid such steep losses in market value in stocks like LegalZoom, we have to ask a pertinent question: can we point to any real evidence in today's data that showcases any of these businesses losing a substantial portion of their revenue? In LegalZoom's case, the company continues to achieve double-digit subscription growth, while its share price has collapsed more than 35% over the past few months. In my view, it's a good time for investors to reassess the bull case on this name.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on LegalZoom in November, when the stock was trading just shy of $10 per share. Of course, this stock has been a meaningful disappointment in my portfolio. That said, I also think the fears surrounding this stock (and the software industry in general) are incredibly disproportionate to the erosion in value that we've seen over the past month, as well as the earnings data that we're actually getting out of a relatively strong Q4 earnings season. With all of these things in mind, I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on LegalZoom.

There are two core reasons to buy LegalZoom, which are business diversification into multiple attractive revenue streams, alongside a cheap valuation. Amid AI fears, it's useful for us to review all the ways in which LegalZoom helps small businesses with their legal and compliance needs. The company's breakout of its total addressable market is shown below:

As shown above, LegalZoom estimates that it has a serviceable addressable market spanning $51 billion. Now, I'm not going to argue that AI won't encroach on any of these territories. There are certainly areas of LegalZoom's business today that are at risk. Business formations, which the company is already leaning away from thanks to their cyclicality, are likely to undergo automation and process improvements as companies deploy agents that can fulfill many of their manual tasks. We also see a lot of the company's current estate planning and entity setup tasks as relatively easier, automatable workflows that can see competition against a generalist AI agent.

But it would be a major stretch to say that all of LegalZoom's business will suddenly come under fire from the threat of AI. In other words, we do not see the entirety of the legal profession being taken over by AI. More complex legal issues, which LegalZoom provides human assistance for (much in the same way that Intuit's (INTU) TurboTax automates much of the manual tax-filing workflow but provides virtual calls with CPAs to handle detailed questions), will likely never be turned over to AI in their entirety. AI cannot negotiate contracts or represent firms in IP lawsuits. Even in some of the company's non-legal work streams, we doubt that companies will use vibe coding to create their own bookkeeping systems and software or automate compliance with local, state, and national regulations and taxing authorities.

AI has certainly made a dent in the professional services world. Fiverr (FVRR), a freelancer marketplace, has seen sharp deceleration in growth and is expecting revenue declines in 2026, thanks in part to AI taking over simpler jobs that were previously handled by hourly freelancers. But we haven't seen any erosion at all in LegalZoom's subscription business, which helps us to take more comfort in its durability.

Despite these advantages, LegalZoom trades at a deep discount to the broader market thanks to this irrational fear. At current post-earnings share prices under $7, LegalZoom trades at a market cap of $1.21 billion. After we net off the $203.1 million of cash (which is free of debt) on the company’s latest balance sheet, LegalZoom’s resulting enterprise value is $1.01 billion.

As shown in the chart above, for FY26 the company is guiding to 8% y/y growth, which represents acceleration versus 3% organic growth in FY25 (yet another indication that the company isn't expecting any AI doomsday). Adjusted EBITDA is also expected in a $190-$200 million range, the midpoint of which reflects a 23.9% adjusted EBITDA margin that is improving 1 point y/y.

This positions LegalZoom's valuation at just 5.2x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. For a business with recurring revenue, that is expecting high single-digit growth and has multiple monetization streams to shield it from any isolated or even AI-driven churn event, I'd say LegalZoom is trading at levels that make it potentially attractive for a private equity buyer. But even standalone, investors who are willing to stomach near-term volatility can benefit from an upward multiples re-rating. In my view, LegalZoom's setup for a rebound in 2026 is strong: if we can get a few quarters under our belts with strong growth, the narrative that AI is an existential threat to this business should fade.

Q4 download

Let's now run through LegalZoom's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

LegalZoom's revenue grew 15% y/y to $190.3 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $184.7 million (+14% y/y) by a one-point margin while also accelerating two points versus 13% y/y growth in Q3. To me, the core item we can continue to lean on for stability is the company's growth in subscription revenue (basically encompassing all the company's recurring services that are not tied to business formations, which it labels as transactional revenue).

As shown in the chart below, subscription revenue (which is now over two-thirds of the business) grew 20% y/y to $190.3 million, outpacing total company growth. The company's total number of subscribers, which it expresses in terms of subscription unit sales, grew 10% y/y to 1.94 million, while average revenue per subscription also grew to $266, reflecting more customers adopting multi-product, higher-spending packages.

It's important to recognize here that LegalZoom was proactive in seeking partnerships with AI companies. Users can access LegalZoom directly from within the ChatGPT and Perplexity interfaces. Rather than viewing AI as a free threat to the company's expertise, LegalZoom is treating AI as a key marketing channel for new customers, and its strategy revolves around keeping a "human in the loop" for critical, complex decisions.

Per CEO Jeffrey Stibel's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

We believe AI tools are accelerating entrepreneurship by lowering barriers to starting and running a business, as evidenced by the data, U.S. business formations have accelerated over the last few quarters. We suspect some of this is anomalous, but we believe AI is a meaningful tailwind. Crucially, this is expanding our addressable market, and we plan to capture more of that market but not with the old software-only playbook. As our market expands, we will leverage AI to continue to lead in what can be automated, but we recognized early that long-term growth cannot come from automation alone. That's why in 2023, we moved our flagship automated formation product to free, choosing to cannibalize our own business before the market did.

Here is the key insight. Defense alone is not a strategy. We believe durable growth will come from what AI cannot automate, nuance, judgment, execution and accountability. Over the past 2 years, we've laid the foundation for the shift by strengthening our subscription business, reorienting our go-to-market strategy to focus on higher-value customers and scaling AI while strategically integrating human experts into the workflow at critical junctures. Better still, a human in the loop also increases conversion and attachment across our automated products because customers move forward with confidence knowing we stand behind that.

On the profitability side, we note that on a full-year basis, LegalZoom's total expenses as a percentage of revenue fell 1 point to 77%, with savings on technology/development and general overhead costs partially offset by increased investments into sales and marketing.

In Q4, adjusted EBITDA grew 13% y/y to $49.9 million, reflecting a 26% full-year adjusted EBITDA margin. Full-year margins rose 1 point y/y to 23%, with the company expecting further margin expansion ahead as top-line leverage and increasing ARPU per subscriber help to offset greater marketing investments.

Key takeaways

There is sure to be a small subset of LegalZoom's current customers and use cases that will get cannibalized by AI. But on the whole, I don't see LegalZoom disappearing any more than I see the entire legal profession fading away. While AI might not be a net-tailwind for LegalZoom as a whole, I believe the AI-driven increase in new businesses and startups requiring the company's ongoing subscription services will help offset any client churn. Meanwhile, the stock's current multiples reflect a much harsher reality of a business that is in near-term danger of extinction: a fear that isn't at all reflected in the strength of LegalZoom's latest Q4 results or its outlook for FY26.

Take advantage of this disconnect as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Ardelyx: Strong IBSRELA Growth, But Heavy Operational Spend Clouds Profitability
data.description:
Thesis: Have to justify a hefty operational spend

Ardelyx, Inc. (ARDX) just reported fourth quarter results and actually beat on revenue, but they missed slightly on earnings, which the market took negatively, as you may know. Q4 2025 revenue came in at $125.25 million, up about 7.9% year over year, a figure that beat estimates by $7.21 million. Revenue here was mainly driven by strong Ibsrela sales. As for earnings, the company’s GAAP EPS of $0.00 missed by $0.01 due to some sharply higher costs, including SG&A, which jumped to $337.2 million, and R&D, to $71.5 million for the year.

Now, management has put this down to the need for scaling commercialization and pipeline programs. FY 2025 showed strong Ibsrela growth but a pretty steep decline in Xphozah revenue, with a net loss widening to $61.6 million. This is one area where the market would have some concerns that possibly expenses are rising faster than profitability despite top-line strength. We also need to take a deep look at what's expected from 2026 with such a big research spend. Ardelyx has the Phase 3 CIC trial and their next-generation NHE3 inhibitor program taking up a large portion of the spend, so it's worth trying to value the longer-term potential in these areas.

FY 2025 earnings

Ardelyx has delivered a pretty notable revenue performance for FY 2025, and the initial market reaction is quite negative. Shares find themselves trading about 8% lower in pre-market today. As for company performance. We saw explosive growth in Ibsrela, which was much needed to offset the hefty contraction in Xphozah. However, I think there was still decent top-line expansion overall. Total revenue jumped to $407.3 million, up from $333.6 million in 2024, which was mainly driven by Ibsrela’s surge to $274.2 million, representing 73% year-over-year growth. Ibsrela now makes up about 82% of the company's revenue. The big chunk came from Q4 2025, where Ibsrela alone contributed $86.6 million, up 61% versus Q4 2024 and about 11% sequentially.

So, that tells us there’s not just annual growth here but accelerating quarterly momentum for that asset. Management mentioned on the call that this is due to deeper penetration among target prescribers and just all-around improved patient engagement in IBS-C. It’s led to much better prescription pull-through, which management is seeing in both volume and adherence improvements.

More importantly is that we see this same momentum in 2026, and on that front, management is guiding for Ibsrela to bring in revenue of $410 to $430 million, which would mean another 50% plus growth year. They’re also projecting for a hefty $1 billion in annual revenue by 2029, which is impressive. Again, Ibsrela is expected to make up the larger chunk of that target and should be regarded as the company’s primary long-term value driver. Keep in mind that this guidance is paired with patent protection through 2042, which, in my opinion, substantially de-risks the franchise from a competitive standpoint.

So on the flip side, Xphozah revenue is taking big hits. Revenue in this area declined to $103.6 million from $160.9 million in 2024, despite Q4 2025 revenue reaching $27.8 million and reported growth in total dispenses. This tells me that pricing, payer mix, or reimbursement headwinds are playing a negative role here, particularly with regard to Medicare populations. There’s clearly a good deal of pressure on recognized revenue even if utilization is expanding. Management’s expectation for 2026 is for revenue of $110 to $120 million, which would just reflect modest growth. So it would seem Xphozah is transitioning into a more mature, slower-growth asset compared to Ibsrela. This was somewhat expected.

Elsewhere on the cost side, Ardelyx increased R&D spending, now up to $71.5 million, about a 37% increase compared to last year, and we can put this down mainly to the launch of the Phase 3 CIC trial (ACCEL) and their next-generation NHE3 inhibitor program (RDX10531). There’s a big strategic reinvestment of commercial cash flows into pipeline expansion here, and I think we can expect much of the potential upside in 2026 to rest in this area, as I'll explain. SG&A also rose pretty sharply, now up to $337.2 million, about 30% up year-over-year. In my opinion, the SG&A cost is massive. To put it in context, it's even higher than FY24 revenue, which is concerning. This would be due to the operational reality of scaling two commercial products, but even so, it is still rather large. There’s a heavy commercialization spend, and it would be the primary reason the company posted a larger net loss of $61.6 million, despite higher revenue.

As for cash, the company holds $264.7 million in cash and short-term investments and is set to grow operating leverage from Ibsrela. The balance sheet appears quite sufficient to fund those near-term trials, but not with the current operational spend. If we see an R&D expense as we did in 2025, then that cash figure would only last about 11 quarters, and that's without including SG&A.

Now, according to the company’s most recent SEC filings, Ardelyx filed an automatic shelf registration back in November last year. So it can offer up to $100 million of common stock, preferred stock, debt securities, warrants, or units over time. So they have the flexibility to raise capital via equity if needed, but it just doesn't seem justified to do so, given what they're making in sales.

Phase 3 ACCEL Trial: Expanding tenapanor’s reach beyond IBS-C

As you know, one of Ardelyx’s key commercial products going forward is tenapanor, which is marketed as Ibsrela in the U.S. The drug essentially works by inhibiting the sodium/hydrogen exchanger isoform 3 (NHE3) in the intestine. Now, we typically see NHE3 as a transporter on the surface of gut cells responsible for absorbing dietary sodium. So in blocking this transporter, tenapanor can increase sodium, and therefore water, in the intestinal lumen.

The benefits of this are very important; patients see softening of stool and accelerated transit, which helps relieve constipation and other abdominal symptoms seen in IBS. However, it also seems to improve visceral sensitivity and reduce intestinal permeability. This effect can help with pain and discomfort associated with bowel dysfunction.

So, with IBS-C already addressed by current approval, what Ardelyx is looking to achieve with the Phase 3 ACCEL trial is to demonstrate that tenapanor can also be effective for chronic idiopathic constipation (CIC). Keep in mind that this is a separate but related disorder that affects tens of millions of adults. It’s often characterized by infrequent or difficult bowel movements and, of course, a markedly reduced quality of life. The ACCEL trial is a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study that’s tipped to enroll about 700 adult patients. The first patient got dosed last month with tenapanor, being taken twice daily over 26 weeks. The primary endpoint for the trial is based on patient-reported outcomes in constipation. So that means it’s designed to specifically show that tenapanor provides meaningful relief from the daily symptoms. This is what matters most to patients for a more improved quality of life.

Now, from a business perspective, CIC would mean a much larger potential market than IBS-C alone. So success in ACCEL could allow Ardelyx to then go on and file a Supplemental New Drug Application with the FDA to expand the label. The end goal being broadening tenapanor’s commercial reach and significantly increasing that long-term revenue potential we’re already seeing from Ibsrela. The downside is that Phase 3 trials are inherently expensive, especially with such a large enrollment number.

So a good deal of upside for the stock would be in Ardelyx succeeding in expanding Ibsrela into CIC. As for the new TAM, the new market would become quite large. You see, CIC affects an estimated 34 million adults in the U.S. (as per the company's press release), and with Ibsrela’s cash list price at about $2,000 to $2,500 per month, that translates to about $24,000 to $30,000 per year. However, we should keep in mind that the net realized pricing is a bit lower due to rebates and assistance programs. So a reasonable modelling assumption is for $20,000 to $25,000 per treated patient per year. So if we see Ibsrela for CIC priced similarly to IBS-C, which is highly likely given identical molecule and dosing, and we also see decent adoption that mirrors Ibsrela’s early IBS-C penetration, the upside could be quite substantial. It would be in the order of about 5% of the target population over time, so based on the CIC U.S. instances, that would imply roughly 1.7 million CIC patients on therapy.

Now, at $22,000 per patient per year, this equates to roughly $37 billion in theory. However, this is only a highly theoretical estimate, and in reality, adoption will be far slower. You see, the TAM for CIC is so large because constipation without the IBS pain component is far more prevalent than IBS-C. Chronic idiopathic constipation affects about 32 million adults in the U.S., versus an estimated 2.4 to 3.5 million patient visits with IBS-C. However, an annual TAM, even at a more conservative scenario of 1% penetration, so 340,000 patients, would still represent about $7.5 billion in annual revenue potential. This is just to show you why Ardelyx may be willing to absorb the high cost of a Phase 3 trial to pursue this label expansion. But keep in mind, this is a very loose model based on the list price of Ibsrela, and in reality that price may differ between patients.

We can dig into the reported 2025 Ibsrela revenue of $274.2 million a bit more. The estimated net price of roughly $20,000 to $25,000 per patient per year would give us about 11,000 to 14,000 patients who are currently on Ibsrela for IBS-C. That means penetration of the IBS-C population today is still well under 1%, showing us just how early the commercial curve still is.

Valuation

When it comes to a valuation, the selloff today has made some metrics screen a bit more attractively, but Ardelyx is still trading as a revenue-scaled commercial biotech rather than a pre-revenue story. The most relevant metrics would be sales-based multiples and forward earnings power. They hold a market cap of about $1.64 billion and an enterprise value of $1.63 billion. Ardelyx trades at about 4.1x trailing EV to sales on $407 million of 2025 revenue and, more importantly, 2.9x forward EV to sales, which is slightly below the healthcare sector median and also dramatically lower than its own five-year average near 15x. So there’s obviously still a lot of investor skepticism around profitability and expense growth, especially when the label expansion of Ibsrela, their new largest growth driver, is far from confirmed. Consensus models for forward GAAP price to earnings show sharp operating leverage emerging, compressing to 11x in 2027 and about 6.6x in 2028.

This setup tells me the stock is being valued mainly on future margin expansion, not current profits, with the market assigning only modest multiple expansion due to the risks of that hefty operational spending. Despite this, they do hold a revenue base growing 50%+ annually. So in short, Ardelyx could be viewed as pretty cheap relative to its historical sales multiple but rather expensive relative to the current earnings figure they just posted. It makes the stock highly sensitive to execution on more Ibsrela growth and cost discipline.

Looking ahead

Long story short, that operational spend seems way too high. It's tipped to be driven by the commercialization of Ibsrela and Xphzoah, plus R&D for the Phase 3 ACCEL trial and next-generation NHE3 program. There's a lot going on; we can give the company that, but we have to think if this spend is strategically justifiable given the potential upside from expanding tenapanor into CIC. Also, Xphzoah sales are leveling out, so it would be better viewing if cost-saving measures were also put in place to offset those areas instead of solely relying on the jump in Ibsrela sales.

So, for me, the company will need to demonstrate better cost management over time for investors to build confidence here. In the near term, expect some stock price turbulence, since revenue growth is strong but margins remain under pressure. Investors are likely best to wait before buying in, especially with the topline Phase 3 ACCEL results expected sometime in 2H27. Successful CIC data could meaningfully de-risk the growth story, but between then and now, I would give this company a Hold rating based on what we just saw on earnings.

data.title: Public Storage: Strong REIT With An A2 Credit Profile, But The Opportunity Is In The Preferreds
data.description:
Public Storage (PSA) remains one of the highest-quality and financially sound REITs. The company has a strong balance sheet, high asset quality, and an investment-grade credit rating. However, at current market valuations, the common stock appears to be more fairly valued, and the more attractive option at present is the preferred stock, which offers current yields exceeding 6% with similar investment-grade credit risk. If you want to see our last article for the company, you can check it out here.

PSA in details

Public Storage (PSA) is one of the largest REIT companies and owners of self-storage facilities. Its current market capitalization is close to $52.85 billion, and the total assets of the last 4th quarter were close to $20.208 billion. The total debt of the company is around $10.53 billion, and the EBITDA is ~ $3.406 billion. PSA has more than 3400 properties across 40 states and close to 243 million rentable square feet. The company has a credit rating of "A2" from Moody's and "A" from S&P.

In the fourth quarter of 2025, PSA reported core FFO of $4.26 per share for the quarter and core FFO for the full year of $16.97 per share. Same-store sales revenue decreased 0.2% year-over-year. Management outlined its PS4.0 initiative, a strategic transformation focused on modernizing operations and improving technology integration. The company also detailed changes to its management and board to support the next phase of growth. These updates can be considered a shift from purely scale-oriented growth to operational optimization and digital efficiency. For 2026, PSA forecasts core FFO of $16.35–$17.00 per share, suggesting potential for future growth. You can see more details here. In our view, PSA is moving into a more normalized environment, but its structural strengths remain intact. The outlook looks stable with potential for future growth.

We will calculate some important financial metrics for the company, using the new information from Q4. The asset yield of PSA ((total revenue - operating expenses + depreciation and amortization) / total assets) is close to 16.86%. The Asset Coverage Ratio (total assets/debt) is ~ 192%. Operating expenses from the total revenue (excluding depreciation and amortization) are around 29.52%. The interest expenses from the total debt are close to 2.89%. We also calculate the market-adjusted asset yield to be more accurate. Therefore, we will need a price-to-book value, which is, at the time of writing, 10.76. We replace the book value of equity with its market value by applying the current p/b. From here, we can calculate the market-adjusted asset yield of around 3.06%, and the market-adjusted asset coverage ratio is the highest in the sector, close to the extremely 1000%. The forward price to AFFO is close to 18.91, so the expected AFFO yield for the company is close to 5.29%. Combined with moderate future growth guidance, this supports that the common stock is reasonably priced. You can see the basic valuation metrics below:

Credibility

We know that PSA has an "A2" credit rating by Moody's, but we want to show you the separate credit metrics of its rating, using Moody's methodology again:

The gross assets of PSA are close to $31.6 billion (the total assets+accumulated depreciation), or equivalent to the highest rating of an "Aaa". The same is with the unencumbered assets (most of the assets are unencumbered) and the secured debt, which is less than 1% - those two metrics are equivalent to an "Aaa" credit rating too. The total debt plus preferred stock as a percentage of the gross assets is close to 46%, which is equivalent to a "Baa3" credit rating. The ratio of the net debt to EBITDA is close to 7.9, which is equivalent to a "Baa1." EBITDA covers interest and preferred stock dividends at a rate of nearly five times, and for fixed charge coverage, the credit rating is equivalent to an "A1." The market positioning, asset quality, and operating environment of PSA receive an "A2" credit rating. And for liquidity and access to capital: "A1."

The overall credit score is an "A2," just like Moody's rates the company, as we mentioned above. But after calculating all ratios based on market-adjusted characteristics, we obtain an "A1" credit rating. But this should be interpreted with caution. Market adjustments can overstate the strength of assets during periods of elevated stock valuations.

Moody's data confirms PSA's structural soundness. The company benefits from scale, high-quality real estate, minimal secured debt, and ongoing fixed-charge coverage.

Below are shown all credit metrics:

Preferred Stocks

PSA's preferred stocks are rated with "BBB+" by S&P and an "A3" by Moody's. Their current yield range is from 5.92%-6.20%. All of them traded below par at the time of writing. Most of the stocks are callable already, except Public Storage 4.100% DP PFD S (PSA.PR.S), Public Storage 3.950% DEP PFD Q (PSA.PR.Q), Public Storage 4% DEP SHS PFD P (PSA.PR.P), and Public Storage 4% DEP PFD SHS R (PSA.PR.R), which have less than a year to call. Below are shown the potential prices and current yield of the rest.

Public Storage 3.875% DEP PFD N (PSA.PR.N): coupon rate 3.88%, annual amount $0.96875, and current yield 6.20%:

Public Storage 4.125 DP SH PF M (PSA.PR.M): coupon rate 4.13%, annual amount $1.03125, and current yield 6.19%:

Public Storage 4.7% DP RP PFD J (PSA.PR.J): coupon rate 4.10%, annual amount $1.18, and current yield 6.19%:

Public Storage 4.75 DP PFD SH K (PSA.PR.K): coupon rate 4.75%, annual amount $1.19, and current yield 6.14%:

Public Storage 3.9% DEP SHS REP 1/1000 PFD BEN INT SR O (PSA.PR.O): coupon rate 3.90%, annual amount $0.975, and current yield 6.13%:

Public Storage 4.875% DP SH PFD (PSA.PR.I): coupon rate 4.88%, annual amount $1.22, and current yield 6.12%:

Public Storage 4.625 DEP PFD L (PSA.PR.L): coupon rate 4.63%, annual amount $1.15625, and current yield 6.11%:

Public Storage PFD SHS F 5.15% (PSA.PR.F): coupon rate 5.15%, annual amount $1.29, and current yield 6.08%:

Public Storage 5.05% CUM PFD G (PSA.PR.G): coupon rate 5.05%, annual amount $1.26, and current yield 5.98%:

Public Storage 5.60 DEPSHS RP H (PSA.PR.H): coupon rate 5.60%, annual amount $1.40, and current yield 5.92%:

OTC Bonds

Below are shown the over-the-counter bonds of PSA:

All are rated with an "A2" credit rating by Moody's. The highest yield from the group is close to 5.45% yield to maturity, with around 0.8% spread to the treasury yield.

In the next chart, we see all the REIT Industrial sector OTC bonds. The highest in the group is only close to 5.51%. PSA's preferred stocks are higher, with close to 0.70%; the stocks are more attractive, especially when they are with a similar investment-grade category credit rating. Additionally, preferred shares have preferential tax treatment for some investors, and the difference in after-tax returns may be even more enticing.

Conclusion

PSA is a stable REIT with a high-quality portfolio, strong market positioning, and an investment-grade credit rating. The preferred stocks offer a yield of around and above 6% (close to 1% more than the sector's bonds). The overall credit picture supports the official A2 rating, but market-adjusted metrics likely overstate the true credit strength. PSA is suitable for long-term conservative investors, but at current levels, we prefer preferred stocks, which offer current yields above 6% with similar credit risk.

data.title: BNY Mellon AMT-Free Municipal Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
“We believe municipal credit conditions should continue to remain stable.”

Market Review

The municipal bond market extended its rally in the fourth quarter, outperforming U.S. Treasuries and corporate bonds, supported by relatively favorable technicals and two additional 25 bp Fed rate cuts. Despite two consecutive quarters of strength, municipal bonds trailed other fixed income sectors for the full year. On the supply side, new issuance accelerated in November and December after a soft October, bringing total 4Q25 issuance to $141 billion, up 12.5% from 4Q24, according to the Bond Buyer. Full-year supply set another new annual record of $580 billion, up 12.9% year over year, driven in part by front-running tax policy uncertainties, higher cost of capital expenditure due to higher inflation, and waning of pandemic-related funds. Taxable municipal issuance remained low at $33 billion in 2025, down 12.4% year over year. In 4Q25, investor demand remained robust and municipal funds saw strong inflows, especially into municipal ETFs. Per Investment Company Institute data, municipal open-ended mutual funds had net inflows of $13 billion, while ETFs gained $45 billion for the whole year 2025.

Performance Summary

For the quarter ended December 31, 2025, the Fund’s Class I shares (DMBIX) returned 1.66%, excluding sales charges. In comparison, the Fund’s unmanaged benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Municipal Bond Index, returned 1.56% for the same period.

The Net Expenses is the actual fund expense ratio applicable to investors. The net expense ratios reflect a contractual expense reduction agreement through 12/31/2025, without which the performance would have been lower. Not all classes of shares may be available to all investors or through all broker-dealer platforms.

Market Review (continued)

During the fourth quarter, the municipal yield curve twisted flatter, with yields from two- to five-year maturities increasing 8 to 19 bps, while yields from ten to thirty years decreased 8 to 27 bps. To the contrary, the treasury yield curve twisted steeper, with yields from one-to-three-year maturities decreasing 1 to 14 bps, while yields from five-to-thirty years increased 5 to 14 bps. As a result, the Muni-Treasury ratio, a key gauge of relative value, cheapened in the short 2-5-year maturity range while richening in the 10-30-year part. The 2-year AAA Muni-to- Treasury ratio increased to 70% from 62%, while the 10-year ratio decreased to 65% from 70% and the 30Y ratio decreased to 85% from 89%, which were at richer side of fair value. In 4Q25, shorter duration municipal bonds underperformed their intermediate and longer counterparts, reflected in the Bloomberg Municipal 3-year Index return of 0.46%, compared to the Bloomberg Municipal 10-year and long Index returns of 1.74% and 1.63% respectively.

From a credit rating perspective during 4Q25, the lowest investment grade segment delivered relatively stronger performance, reflected by the Bloomberg Municipal BBB Index return of 1.77%, outperforming the Bloomberg Municipal AAA, AA & A Index returns of 1.57%, 1.57%, and 1.50% respectively. Within sectors, general obligation (GO) bonds outperformed revenue bonds slightly. Hospital, housing, and education sub-sectors were strong performers during the quarter, while IDR, tobacco, and special tax sub-sectors were generally weaker performers. Municipal credit spreads widened across the quality spectrum in 4Q25. The Bloomberg Municipal High-yield Index returned +1.15% over the quarter, underperforming the investment-grade Bloomberg Municipal index, which returned +1.56%. The Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Index returned +1.37% during the fourth quarter, outperformed the Bloomberg US Treasury Index return of +0.90% and the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Index return of +0.84%.

Performance Review

The Fund’s long duration stance and decisions to overweight 10- to 30-year part of the curve contributed to positive outperformance with yields on the long end rallying during the quarter. Security selection and sector allocation were muted for the period.

Market Outlook

Municipal bonds finished the year strongly in 4Q25 with positive returns that outpaced treasuries while the bond market overall delivered the best annual returns since 2020. A catalyst was the US Federal Reserve’s (the “Fed”) dovish rate cuts of 25 bp each during both October and November. Annual supply set a record near $580 billion, up 14% year over year. Looking ahead, 2026 is expected to be another heavy supply year driven by growing needs to fund aging infrastructure, higher cost of capital expenditure, and waning of pandemic-aid. Municipal tax-equivalent yields for top tax rate investors continue to look attractive versus high-grade fixed income alternatives. This should continue to fuel solid demand from individual investors via mutual fund and ETF flows as well as direct purchasing support separately managed account growth.

We believe municipal credit conditions should continue to remain stable, supported by very high reserves and cash balances that have accumulated in recent years and modest economic growth supporting tax revenues. Many segments of the market are facing credit headwinds as we head into 2026. Tariffs, federal job cuts, and the mass deportation of illegal immigrants are likely to have negative impacts on economic growth, which could lead to some softness in income and sales tax receipts. At the same time, cuts in federal spending in areas such as Medicaid, “green” infrastructure initiatives, and federal disaster relief could pressure state/local budgets. The likely expiration of healthcare subsidies is a credit negative for hospitals, especially those operating in rural areas, while tuition affordability concerns combined with weak demographic trends are pressuring smaller colleges with weak endowments.

From a sector standpoint, we maintain core positions in state and local general obligation, utilities (water and power), public higher education, local housing, and transportation credits, which benefit from essential service demand, broad and reliable revenue streams, and in many cases explicit or implicit government support. We remain cautious on hospital bonds amid margin pressures and anticipated Medicaid cuts, with a tilt toward larger healthcare systems with dominant positions in growing markets, strong balance sheets, and those less reliant on government payors.

Although federal policy shifts under the current administration have introduced some uncertainty for municipal credit, most sectors appear to be positioned to absorb potential pressure. That said, major rating agencies have assigned negative outlooks for 2026 to select sectors, notably higher education and seaports. We recommend a more selective, risk aware approach—prioritizing balance sheet strength, liquidity, and operating flexibility. While mainland hurricane activity was absent in 2025, integrating climate risk into investment decisions remains essential.

Our duration stance is neutral-to-modestly long versus our benchmarks. During 2025, bonds maturing 15 years and beyond underperformed shorter maturities, steepening the yield curve and creating compelling value out toward the long end. We believe this segment can produce attractive relative returns as demand is likely to pick up under a more accommodative Federal Reserve. A barbell curve posture may be favorable with exposure to short maturities, which should benefit from further Fed easing while longer maturity bonds offer attractive yield in a relatively steep curve environment.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus, or a summary prospectus, if available, that contains this and other information about a fund, contact your financial professional or visit bny.com/investments. Read the prospectus carefully before investing. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Risks

Bonds are subject to interest-rate, credit, liquidity, call and market risks, to varying degrees. Generally, all other factors being equal, bond prices are inversely related to interest-rate changes and rate increases can cause price declines.

Municipal income may be subject to state and local taxes. Some income may be subject to the federal alternative minimum tax for certain investors. U.S. Treasuries while the U.S. Government guarantees the timely payment of principal and interest on its securities, portfolios that invest in such securities are not guaranteed and will fluctuate in value. Capital gains, if any, are taxable. Portfolio composition is subject to change at any time.

Asset allocation and diversification cannot ensure a profit or protect against loss in declining markets.

The Bloomberg US Municipal Bond Index is a widely accepted, unmanaged total return performance benchmark for the long-term, investment-grade, tax-exempt bond market. Reflects investments of dividends and, where applicable, capital gain distributions. The Bloomberg US Treasury Index measures US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate, nominal debt issued by the US Treasury. Treasury bills are excluded by the maturity constraint but are part of a separate Short Treasury Index. The Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Index measures the performance of the investment-grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market within the United States. The Bloomberg Municipal Managed Money Long Index is a rules-based, market-value-weighted index that measures the performance of publicly traded, USD-denominated, long-term, tax-exempt municipal bonds, including general obligation bonds, revenue bonds, insured bonds, and pre-refunded bonds. The Bloomberg 3-Year Municipal Bond Index is a benchmark that tracks the performance of a specific segment of the municipal bond market. It includes fixed-rate, investment-grade, tax-exempt bonds with remaining maturities between two and four years. The Bloomberg 1-10 Year Municipal Bond Index tracks the performance of investment-grade municipal bonds with maturities between 1 and 10 years. Bloomberg Municipal Bond AAA, AA, A and BBB Indices are subsets of the Bloomberg Barclays Municipal Bond Index. The Bloomberg Municipal High Yield Index is a benchmark that tracks the performance of the non-investment grade, tax-exempt municipal bond market in the United States. It includes bonds that are rated below investment grade (Ba1 or lower) or are unrated and are U.S. dollar-denominated and fixed-rate. The Bloomberg Municipal Taxable Bonds Index measures the performance of the U.S. municipal taxable investment-grade bond market. It includes bonds issued by state and local governments that are subject to federal income tax. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

A yield curve is a line that plots the yields or interest rates of bonds that have equal credit quality but different maturity dates. The slope of the yield curve predicts the direction of interest rates and the economic expansion or contraction that could result.

Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond or other debt instrument to a change in interest rates. A bond's duration is easily confused with its term or time to maturity because they are both measured in years. However, a bond's term is a linear measure of the years until repayment of principal is due; it does not change with the interest rate environment. Duration, on the other hand is non-linear and accelerates as time to maturity lessens. Dovish describes a monetary policy stance that favors lower interest rates to stimulate economic growth and potentially increase employment, even if it means accepting a higher level of inflation. The Muni/Treasury ratio is a measure that compares the yield of municipal bonds to that of U.S. Treasury bonds. It's a key metric for assessing the relative value of municipal bonds, particularly for investors considering the tax benefits of munis.

Definitions

Q is quarter. NAV is Net Asset Value. YTD is Year to Date. FDIC is Federal Deposit Insurance Corp.
